conversations.content: What is affecting this plant's leaves?

This cashew leaf shows signs of **Anthracnose**, caused by *Colletotrichum gloeosporioides*. Dark, sunken lesions on leaves, twigs, and nuts. Leaves show irregular brown-black spots that may merge, causing defoliation. Flower blight reduces nut set. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive necrosis, heavy defoliation, flower blight, and nut drop.
What's the best way to control this?

For anthracnose in cashew: Prune and destroy infected branches. Apply copper-based fungicides (e.g., Bordeaux mixture) preventively before flowering. Use systemic fungicides like carbendazim or mancozeb during wet seasons. Ensure proper spacing for air circulation.
How can I prevent this in the future?
To prevent anthracnose: Plant resistant varieties. Maintain canopy hygiene by removing fallen debris. Avoid overhead irrigation. Apply fungicides at early flowering stage.

What preventive measures work against this?

To prevent fall armyworm damage: Plant early to avoid peak armyworm season. Use Bt-maize varieties where available. Scout fields weekly during vegetative stage. Encourage natural enemies (parasitic wasps, birds). Use pheromone traps for monitoring.

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/leaf blight/1328maize_train_leaf blight.JPG

conversations.content: What is affecting this plant's leaves?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Exserohilum turcicum (Northern Corn Leaf Blight) or Bipolaris maydis (Southern Corn Leaf Blight)*. Long, elliptical, grayish-green to tan lesions on leaves. Northern type: cigar-shaped lesions 3-15 cm long. Southern type: smaller, tan, rectangular lesions. Lesions may coalesce, killing entire leaves. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: a few lesions on lower leaves only.
How do I manage this disease?

For leaf blight in maize: Apply foliar fungicides (azoxystrobin, propiconazole, or pyraclostrobin) at first signs of disease above the ear leaf. Timing is critical — application before tasseling gives best ROI.

label: maize_leaf_blight

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Cassava/train_set/bacterial blight/7016cassava_train_bb.JPG

conversations.content: What condition is this plant suffering from?

This cassava leaf shows signs of **Bacterial Blight**, caused by *Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. manihotis*. Angular, water-soaked leaf spots that turn brown. Wilting and drooping of shoot tips. Gum exudation from stems. In severe cases, complete shoot dieback. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: widespread wilting, stem gum exudation, shoot dieback, plant collapse.
How should I treat this?

For bacterial blight in cassava: Remove and burn infected plant parts immediately. There are no effective chemical treatments — management is primarily cultural. Use disease-free planting material. Rotate crops to break disease cycle.
How can I prevent this in the future?

To prevent bacterial blight: Use certified disease-free stem cuttings. Plant resistant varieties (e.g., TME 419, IITA-improved lines). Avoid working in fields when plants are wet. Practice crop rotation with non-host crops for 2-3 seasons.

label: cassava_bacterial_blight

How can I prevent this in the future?

To prevent leaf beetle damage: Rotate crops (breaks rootworm beetle cycle). Use seed-treated varieties. Monitor beetle populations with yellow sticky traps. Maintain healthy, vigorous plants to tolerate moderate damage.

conversations.content: Is this plant diseased? If so, what is it?

This cassava leaf shows signs of **Green Mite Damage**, caused by *Mononychellus tanajoa (spider mite)*. Pale yellow spotting (chlorosis) on young leaves. Leaves become reduced in size and distorted. Growing points may appear bushy due to shortened internodes. In dry conditions, leaves may appear scorched. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: light chlorotic speckling on newest leaves, plant growth unaffected.
How can I save this crop?

For green mite damage in cassava: Introduce biological control agents (predatory mites like Typhlodromalus aripo). Apply sulfur-based or abamectin miticides for severe outbreaks. Irrigate plants during dry spells to reduce mite pressure.
How do I keep my crops safe from this?

To prevent green mite damage: Use resistant/tolerant varieties. Plant early in the rainy season to establish before dry-season mite buildup. Avoid carrying infested planting material between fields. Conserve natural enemies.

label: cassava_green_mite

conversations.content: What is affecting this plant's leaves?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Exserohilum turcicum (Northern Corn Leaf Blight) or Bipolaris maydis (Southern Corn Leaf Blight)*. Long, elliptical, grayish-green to tan lesions on leaves. Northern type: cigar-shaped lesions 3-15 cm long. Southern type: smaller, tan, rectangular lesions. Lesions may coalesce, killing entire leaves. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: lesions on multiple leaf levels, some coalescing.
What treatment do you recommend?

For leaf blight in maize: Apply foliar fungicides (azoxystrobin, propiconazole, or pyraclostrobin) at first signs of disease above the ear leaf. Timing is critical — application before tasseling gives best ROI.

label: maize_leaf_blight

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/fall armyworm/846maize_train_farmyw.JPG

conversations.content: Can you identify what's wrong with this plant?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Fall Armyworm Damage**, caused by *Spodoptera frugiperda (insect pest)*. Ragged feeding damage on leaves with visible frass (excrement). Larvae bore into the whorl, causing 'windowpane' effect on leaves. Older larvae may bore into ears and tassels. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive defoliation, larvae in most whorls, potential ear damage.

label: maize_fall_armyworm

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/test_set/septoria leaf spot/1057tomato_valid_septoria leaf spot.JPG

conversations.content: Diagnose this plant disease from the photo.

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Septoria Leaf Spot**, caused by *Septoria lycopersici*. Numerous small (2-5 mm), circular spots with dark brown borders and gray centers. Tiny black dots (pycnidia) visible in spot centers under magnification. Starts on lower leaves and moves upward. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive defoliation, spots reaching upper canopy, fruit exposed to sunscald.
What should I spray on this plant?

For septoria leaf spot in tomato: Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper. Begin applications at first sign of disease and repeat every 7-10 days. Remove and destroy infected lower leaves to slow spread.
What should I do to avoid this disease next season?

To prevent septoria leaf spot: Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash. Avoid overhead watering. Stake plants for air circulation. Rotate crops — do not plant tomatoes in same spot for 3 years. Remove all plant debris at end of season.

label: tomato_septoria_leaf_spot

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/train_set/verticulium wilt/482tomato_train_verticulium wilt.JPG

conversations.content: Look at this leaf — what disease is it?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Verticillium Wilt**, caused by *Verticillium dahliae*. Yellowing (chlorosis) starts on one side of the leaf or plant ('half-leaf' symptom). Lower leaves wilt and turn brown while upper leaves remain green initially. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: one-sided yellowing and wilting on several branches, reduced vigor.
How should I treat this?

For verticillium wilt in tomato: No effective chemical treatment once plants are infected. Remove and destroy infected plants. Solarize soil in affected areas (cover with clear plastic for 4-6 weeks in hot weather). Add beneficial fungi (Trichoderma) to soil.

label: tomato_verticulium_wilt

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Cashew/test_set/anthracnose/949cashew_valid_anthracnose.JPG

conversations.content: What condition is this plant suffering from?

This cashew leaf shows signs of **Anthracnose**, caused by *Colletotrichum gloeosporioides*. Dark, sunken lesions on leaves, twigs, and nuts. Leaves show irregular brown-black spots that may merge, causing defoliation. Flower blight reduces nut set. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: multiple merging lesions covering 10-40% of leaf area, some twig dieback visible.
What's the best way to control this?

For anthracnose in cashew: Prune and destroy infected branches. Apply copper-based fungicides (e.g., Bordeaux mixture) preventively before flowering. Use systemic fungicides like carbendazim or mancozeb during wet seasons. Ensure proper spacing for air circulation.
How can I prevent this in the future?
To prevent anthracnose: Plant resistant varieties. Maintain canopy hygiene by removing fallen debris. Avoid overhead irrigation. Apply fungicides at early flowering stage.

label: cashew_anthracnose

conversations.content: Diagnose this plant disease from the photo.

This cashew leaf shows signs of **Anthracnose**, caused by *Colletotrichum gloeosporioides*. Dark, sunken lesions on leaves, twigs, and nuts. Leaves show irregular brown-black spots that may merge, causing defoliation. Flower blight reduces nut set. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive necrosis, heavy defoliation, flower blight, and nut drop.
How can I save this crop?

For anthracnose in cashew: Prune and destroy infected branches. Apply copper-based fungicides (e.g., Bordeaux mixture) preventively before flowering. Use systemic fungicides like carbendazim or mancozeb during wet seasons. Ensure proper spacing for air circulation.
How can I prevent this in the future?
To prevent anthracnose: Plant resistant varieties. Maintain canopy hygiene by removing fallen debris. Avoid overhead irrigation. Apply fungicides at early flowering stage.

label: cashew_anthracnose

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Cassava/train_set/mosaic/3021cassava_train_mosaic.JPG

conversations.content: I found this in my field. What's the problem?

This cassava leaf shows signs of **Mosaic Disease**, caused by *Cassava Mosaic Geminiviruses (CMGs), transmitted by whitefly (Bemisia tabaci)*. Distorted leaves with mosaic pattern of yellow and green patches. Leaf size reduction and curling. Stunted plant growth. Reduced tuber yield. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: clear mosaic on multiple leaves, noticeable size reduction and some curling.
What treatment do you recommend?

For mosaic disease in cassava: No chemical cure exists. Remove and destroy severely infected plants (roguing). Replant with virus-free, resistant cuttings. Control whitefly vectors with neem-based insecticides or yellow sticky traps.

label: cassava_mosaic

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/train_set/verticulium wilt/94tomato_train_verticulium wilt.JPG

conversations.content: Can you identify what's wrong with this plant?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Verticillium Wilt**, caused by *Verticillium dahliae*. Yellowing (chlorosis) starts on one side of the leaf or plant ('half-leaf' symptom). Lower leaves wilt and turn brown while upper leaves remain green initially. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: one-sided yellowing and wilting on several branches, reduced vigor.
How do I manage this disease?

For verticillium wilt in tomato: No effective chemical treatment once plants are infected. Remove and destroy infected plants. Solarize soil in affected areas (cover with clear plastic for 4-6 weeks in hot weather). Add beneficial fungi (Trichoderma) to soil.
How do I keep my crops safe from this?

To prevent verticillium wilt: Plant resistant varieties (look for 'V' designation). Practice long crop rotation (4+ years without solanaceous crops). Maintain soil pH around 6.5-7.0. Avoid overwatering and ensure good drainage.

label: tomato_verticulium_wilt

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/leaf blight/1942maize_train_leaf blight.JPG

conversations.content: Diagnose this plant disease from the photo.

This maize leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Exserohilum turcicum (Northern Corn Leaf Blight) or Bipolaris maydis (Southern Corn Leaf Blight)*. Long, elliptical, grayish-green to tan lesions on leaves. Northern type: cigar-shaped lesions 3-15 cm long. Southern type: smaller, tan, rectangular lesions. Lesions may coalesce, killing entire leaves. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: a few lesions on lower leaves only.
What treatment do you recommend?

For leaf blight in maize: Apply foliar fungicides (azoxystrobin, propiconazole, or pyraclostrobin) at first signs of disease above the ear leaf. Timing is critical — application before tasseling gives best ROI.

label: maize_leaf_blight

Lower leaves wilt and turn brown while upper leaves remain green initially. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive wilting, most lower leaves dead, stunting, reduced fruit production.
How do I manage this disease?

For verticillium wilt in tomato: No effective chemical treatment once plants are infected. Remove and destroy infected plants. Solarize soil in affected areas (cover with clear plastic for 4-6 weeks in hot weather). Add beneficial fungi (Trichoderma) to soil.

conversations.content: Look at this leaf — what disease is it?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Septoria Leaf Spot**, caused by *Septoria lycopersici*. Numerous small (2-5 mm), circular spots with dark brown borders and gray centers. Tiny black dots (pycnidia) visible in spot centers under magnification. Starts on lower leaves and moves upward. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive defoliation, spots reaching upper canopy, fruit exposed to sunscald.
What treatment do you recommend?

For septoria leaf spot in tomato: Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper. Begin applications at first sign of disease and repeat every 7-10 days. Remove and destroy infected lower leaves to slow spread.
What preventive measures work against this?

To prevent septoria leaf spot: Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash. Avoid overhead watering. Stake plants for air circulation. Rotate crops — do not plant tomatoes in same spot for 3 years. Remove all plant debris at end of season.

How should I treat this?

For fall armyworm damage in maize: Apply Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) sprays for early-stage larvae. Use emamectin benzoate or chlorantraniliprole for larger larvae. Apply into the whorl where larvae hide. Handpick larvae in small plots.

label: maize_fall_armyworm

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/train_set/leaf blight/3495tomato_train_leaf blight.JPG

conversations.content: Can you identify what's wrong with this plant?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Alternaria solani (Early Blight) or Phytophthora infestans (Late Blight)*. Early blight: concentric ring ('target spot') lesions, starting on lower leaves. Late blight: large, water-soaked, dark brown lesions with white mold on undersides in humid conditions. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: lesions on multiple leaf levels, some defoliation of lower canopy.
How can I save this crop?

For leaf blight in tomato: Early blight: apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin. Late blight: apply metalaxyl/mancozeb immediately — late blight spreads extremely fast. Remove and destroy infected plant material. Do not compost infected tissue.
How can I prevent this in the future?
To prevent leaf blight: Stake and prune for air circulation. Mulch to prevent soil splash. Water in the morning at soil level. Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for 3+ years. Use resistant varieties.

label: tomato_leaf_blight

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/train_set/verticulium wilt/1373tomato_train_verticulium wilt.JPG

conversations.content: I found this in my field. What's the problem?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Verticillium Wilt**, caused by *Verticillium dahliae*. Yellowing (chlorosis) starts on one side of the leaf or plant ('half-leaf' symptom). Lower leaves wilt and turn brown while upper leaves remain green initially. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: one-sided yellowing and wilting on several branches, reduced vigor.
What treatment do you recommend?

For verticillium wilt in tomato: No effective chemical treatment once plants are infected. Remove and destroy infected plants. Solarize soil in affected areas (cover with clear plastic for 4-6 weeks in hot weather). Add beneficial fungi (Trichoderma) to soil.
What preventive measures work against this?

To prevent verticillium wilt: Plant resistant varieties (look for 'V' designation). Practice long crop rotation (4+ years without solanaceous crops). Maintain soil pH around 6.5-7.0. Avoid overwatering and ensure good drainage.

label: tomato_verticulium_wilt

conversations.content: Is this plant diseased? If so, what is it?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Alternaria solani (Early Blight) or Phytophthora infestans (Late Blight)*. Early blight: concentric ring ('target spot') lesions, starting on lower leaves. Late blight: large, water-soaked, dark brown lesions with white mold on undersides in humid conditions. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: lesions on multiple leaf levels, some defoliation of lower canopy.
What should I spray on this plant?

What should I do to avoid this disease next season?
To prevent leaf blight: Stake and prune for air circulation. Mulch to prevent soil splash. Water in the morning at soil level. Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for 3+ years. Use resistant varieties.

For verticillium wilt in tomato: No effective chemical treatment once plants are infected. Remove and destroy infected plants. Solarize soil in affected areas (cover with clear plastic for 4-6 weeks in hot weather). Add beneficial fungi (Trichoderma) to soil.

label: tomato_verticulium_wilt

conversations.content: What condition is this plant suffering from?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Maize Streak Virus**, caused by *Maize Streak Virus (MSV), transmitted by leafhopper (Cicadulina mbila)*. Characteristic parallel streaks of pale yellow along leaf veins. Streaks are narrow and discontinuous. Stunted plant growth. Young plants are most severely affected. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: clear streaking on multiple leaves, some growth reduction.
What should I spray on this plant?
For maize streak virus in maize: No chemical cure. Remove and destroy severely infected plants. Control leafhopper vectors with pyrethroid insecticides. Focus management on early-stage protection.

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/train_set/leaf blight/3276tomato_train_leaf blight.JPG

conversations.content: What is affecting this plant's leaves?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Alternaria solani (Early Blight) or Phytophthora infestans (Late Blight)*. Early blight: concentric ring ('target spot') lesions, starting on lower leaves. Late blight: large, water-soaked, dark brown lesions with white mold on undersides in humid conditions. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: rapid spread to upper canopy, fruit infection, potential total crop loss (especially late blight).
How can I save this crop?

For leaf blight in tomato: Early blight: apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin. Late blight: apply metalaxyl/mancozeb immediately — late blight spreads extremely fast. Remove and destroy infected plant material. Do not compost infected tissue.
What preventive measures work against this?
To prevent leaf blight: Stake and prune for air circulation. Mulch to prevent soil splash. Water in the morning at soil level. Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for 3+ years. Use resistant varieties.

label: tomato_leaf_blight

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/fall armyworm/981maize_train_farmyw.JPG

conversations.content: How bad is this infection?
This is a severe case. Immediate action is needed to prevent further spread and limit crop loss. Extensive defoliation, larvae in most whorls, potential ear damage.
How can I save this crop?

For fall armyworm damage in maize: Apply Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) sprays for early-stage larvae. Use emamectin benzoate or chlorantraniliprole for larger larvae. Apply into the whorl where larvae hide. Handpick larvae in small plots.

label: maize_fall_armyworm

conversations.content: What condition is this plant suffering from?

This cassava leaf shows signs of **Green Mite Damage**, caused by *Mononychellus tanajoa (spider mite)*. Pale yellow spotting (chlorosis) on young leaves. Leaves become reduced in size and distorted. Growing points may appear bushy due to shortened internodes. In dry conditions, leaves may appear scorched. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: light chlorotic speckling on newest leaves, plant growth unaffected.
How can I save this crop?

For green mite damage in cassava: Introduce biological control agents (predatory mites like Typhlodromalus aripo). Apply sulfur-based or abamectin miticides for severe outbreaks. Irrigate plants during dry spells to reduce mite pressure.
What preventive measures work against this?

To prevent green mite damage: Use resistant/tolerant varieties. Plant early in the rainy season to establish before dry-season mite buildup. Avoid carrying infested planting material between fields. Conserve natural enemies.

label: cassava_green_mite

For brown leaf spot in cassava: Apply copper-based fungicides or mancozeb. Remove and destroy severely infected lower leaves. Improve air circulation by adjusting plant spacing.

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/grasshoper/2065maize_train_grasshoper.JPG

conversations.content: What condition is this plant suffering from?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Grasshopper Damage**, caused by *Various grasshopper species (Orthoptera)*. Irregular, chewed leaf margins. Leaves may be stripped to the midrib in severe cases. Feeding starts from leaf edges inward. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: minor leaf edge feeding on scattered plants.
How do I manage this disease?

For grasshopper damage in maize: Apply carbaryl or malathion for severe outbreaks. Use bait formulations (bran + insecticide) along field borders. Biological control with Nosema locustae (grasshopper pathogen). Hand-collect in small fields.
How can I prevent this in the future?

To prevent grasshopper damage: Maintain clean field borders to reduce egg-laying sites. Early planting reduces overlap with grasshopper hatching season. Encourage birds and other natural predators. Till soil after harvest to expose eggs.

label: maize_grasshoper
